label: Principle of relativity
dominant_mnemonic: Identical laws in relatively moving inertial frames
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to two identical S and S' coordinate frames plus one relative-velocity arrow v. Retain one matching internal straight test trace in each frame so the icon still says same experiment, same laws.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic first as two equal inertial laboratories S and S', each with the same x/y axes and the same simple internal experiment: a straight particle trace or identical ruler/tick marks. Put one clean relative-velocity arrow v between the laboratories. The two frames should look equally legitimate and equally detailed; only their relative motion differs.
physics_idea: The principle of relativity says the laws of physics have the same form in every inertial frame; uniform relative motion does not single out an absolute rest frame.
avoid: Acceleration, forces, curved paths, engines, rockets, planets, clocks, or making one frame larger, richer, or more privileged than the other.
icon_caption: Two equal inertial frames and one relative-velocity arrow: the icon says neither frame is privileged.
detail_caption: The same simple internal experiment appears in two uniformly moving inertial frames, emphasizing that the laws have the same form in both.

label: Constancy of the speed of light
dominant_mnemonic: Same circular light wavefront for all inertial observers
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to one central source, three concentric blue wavefronts, and two radial rays labelled c. The invariant circular wavefront is the icon.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a central light flash with expanding circular wavefronts, plus two or three small peripheral inertial-frame markers sampling the wavefront in different directions. Each marker should point radially to the same wavefront with a small c label, making the invariant speed the lesson. The observer markers are secondary; the circular wavefront remains central.
physics_idea: The constancy of the speed of light says light in vacuum has the same speed c for all inertial observers, independent of source or observer motion.
avoid: Material media, mirrors, prisms, lenses, stars, rockets, detailed people, or anything suggesting direction-dependent or observer-dependent light speed.
icon_caption: Concentric wavefronts and repeated c labels: light speed is the same in different directions.
detail_caption: A central flash emits circular wavefronts sampled by different inertial markers; each radial ray represents the same invariant speed c.

label: Inertial frames
dominant_mnemonic: Free particle moves in a straight line at constant velocity
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to one x/y frame, a straight green particle track, equally spaced dots, and a velocity arrow v.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a coordinate frame containing a free particle shown at several equally spaced positions along one straight line. Add faint equal-time tick marks perpendicular to the track and a constant-velocity arrow v. The equal spacing and straightness are the teaching points: no force and no acceleration in an inertial frame.
physics_idea: An inertial frame is one in which a free particle moves with constant velocity unless acted on by forces.
avoid: Gravitational fields, curved paths, circular motion, springs, engines, rockets, clocks, or force arrows.
icon_caption: A straight track with equal positions shows free motion at constant velocity.
detail_caption: The equal spacing along a straight line inside one coordinate frame indicates no force and no acceleration in an inertial frame.

label: Spacetime event
dominant_mnemonic: One occurrence specified by where and when
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to x/ct axes, one red event P, and faint coordinate projections. Place P away from any 45-degree line so it does not look lightlike or special.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a spacetime coordinate diagram with one red event P at an arbitrary non-45-degree location. Add dashed projections to x and ct axes and small coordinate-corner markers to show that an event is specified by both where and when. Keep it as a single occurrence, not a worldline or process.
physics_idea: A spacetime event is one occurrence specified by both position and time; different observers may assign coordinates to the same event.
avoid: Worldlines, light cones, multiple events, clocks, observers, equations, or placing P on a 45-degree light ray unless lightlike separation is the topic.
icon_caption: One red point marks a single spacetime event with x and ct coordinates.
detail_caption: The event P is a single occurrence; dashed projections show how one frame assigns position and time coordinates to it.

label: Principle of locality
dominant_mnemonic: Interactions use only nearby quantities at one event
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to faint x/ct axes, a dashed local neighbourhood, a central red event, and several short arrows ending at that event.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a central event inside a small dashed neighbourhood, with short arrows from nearby field values or neighbouring events terminating at the central event. Add one faint unconnected distant dot outside the neighbourhood to show what is not allowed: instantaneous distant action. The active arrows must all be short and local.
physics_idea: The principle of locality says physical interactions at an event depend on quantities defined at that event or infinitesimally nearby, not on instantaneous distant action.
avoid: Instantaneous long-distance action, distant force arrows, planets, magnets, or particles acting across empty space.
icon_caption: Short arrows meet at one event inside a local neighbourhood.
detail_caption: Only nearby quantities feed the central event, illustrating locality rather than instantaneous influence from distant points.

label: Lorentz transformations
dominant_mnemonic: Same event described by tilted coordinate frames
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to black x/ct axes, blue tilted x'/ct' axes, and one red event P. Keep the tilted primed axes as the icon silhouette.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as one spacetime diagram containing unprimed x/ct axes and tilted primed x'/ct' axes sharing the same origin. Place a red event P at a generic non-45-degree location. Add dashed projection lines from P to both coordinate systems, showing that the same event receives different coordinate descriptions. Add very faint light-cone guide lines through the origin only if they help show preserved structure without dominating.
physics_idea: Lorentz transformations do not move the physical event; they change the coordinate description between inertial frames while preserving spacetime structure.
avoid: Letting light-cone guides dominate, full equations, clocks, observers, rockets, or placing P on a 45-degree light ray.
icon_caption: Tilted primed axes and unprimed axes describe the same event P.
detail_caption: Dashed projections show that Lorentz transformations change coordinate descriptions while leaving the event itself unchanged.

label: Spacetime interval
dominant_mnemonic: Invariant classification of separations
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to two red events A and B connected by one bold blue interval segment labelled s^2. Use a non-45-degree separation for the icon.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a spacetime diagram with a central event O and three example separations: a timelike interval inside the light cone labelled s^2 > 0, a spacelike interval outside the light cone labelled s^2 < 0, and a lightlike interval on the cone labelled s^2 = 0. Use light-cone guide lines and make the three classifications visually distinct. The timelike/spacelike example points should be deliberately away from accidental 45-degree placement.
physics_idea: The spacetime interval is the invariant separation between events; its sign classifies timelike, spacelike, and lightlike relationships independent of coordinate description.
avoid: Making coordinate projection legs more prominent than the invariant separations, writing the full interval formula, or accidentally placing arbitrary event pairs on 45-degree light rays.
icon_caption: Two events joined by s^2 represent an invariant spacetime separation.
detail_caption: Three separations from one event show the interval sign classes: timelike, spacelike, and lightlike.

label: Metric tensor
dominant_mnemonic: Metric as the spacetime measuring rule
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to an eta tile applied to x/ct axes, with one blue time-like mark and one grey space-like mark.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as an actual compact 4x4 diagonal Minkowski metric matrix labelled eta_mu_nu, with +1 in the time slot and -1 entries in the three spatial slots. Pair it with a small spacetime-axis inset showing a blue time-like component and grey space-like component being measured by the matrix. The matrix should be readable in the panel but reducible to the eta tile in the icon.
physics_idea: The metric tensor is the rule that turns coordinate components into invariant spacetime lengths and inner products. It distinguishes the time direction from space directions.
avoid: A dense derivation, tensor-index clutter beyond the metric label, or anything that looks like an ordinary Euclidean ruler with all directions equivalent.
icon_caption: The eta tile and time/space marks indicate the metric as a measuring rule.
detail_caption: The parenthesized Minkowski metric displays +1 for time and -1 for space, with a small axis inset showing what it measures.

label: Light cone
dominant_mnemonic: Causal boundary from one event
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to one central red event and four 45-degree blue light rays forming future and past cones on x/ct axes.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a pure spacetime diagram with one central event, future and past light-cone boundaries, lightly shaded future/past causal regions, and clearly separate spacelike side regions. Add small labels such as future, past, or c only if they remain unobtrusive. This is the concept where 45-degree lines are intentional and should dominate.
physics_idea: The light cone divides spacetime into causally reachable and spacelike-separated regions.
avoid: Extra worldlines, multiple arbitrary events, tilted coordinate frames, or visual clutter. This one should remain geometrically pure.
icon_caption: Four 45-degree rays form the causal light cone from one event.
detail_caption: The shaded cone separates future and past causal regions from spacelike regions; the c label marks the lightlike boundary.

label: Minkowski diagram
dominant_mnemonic: Spacetime plotting board with worldlines
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to x/ct axes with three worldlines: black vertical rest line, green slanted massive-particle line, and one blue 45-degree light ray labelled c.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a working spacetime plot: x/ct axes, faint grid, a vertical rest worldline, a slanted sub-light massive-particle worldline, and one 45-degree light ray labelled c. Add arrowheads upward in time and a couple of event dots on the worldlines. Ensure the massive-particle line is visibly not at 45 degrees so it is not confused with light.
physics_idea: A Minkowski diagram is the visual tool for comparing events, worldlines, light propagation, and relative motion in spacetime.
avoid: Making it look like the light-cone graphic. Use one light ray, not the full cone; avoid placing massive-particle worldlines at 45 degrees.
icon_caption: A Minkowski plot combines a rest line, a massive-particle line, and a light ray.
detail_caption: The grid and event dots make this a working spacetime diagram for comparing worldlines and light propagation.

label: Proper time
dominant_mnemonic: Clock ticks accumulated along a worldline
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to one non-lightlike worldline from A to B with short tick marks along it and label tau. Keep coordinate-time projection out of the icon unless there is room.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a spacetime diagram with two events A and B joined by a timelike worldline that is deliberately not at 45 degrees. Draw several small clock ticks along the worldline and a faint vertical coordinate-time projection on the ct axis, so the contrast is between elapsed proper time along the path and coordinate time in a chosen frame. If a second faint alternative path is included, keep it secondary and use it only to show path dependence of proper time.
physics_idea: Proper time is the invariant time measured by a clock travelling along a particular worldline between events. It belongs to the path, not to one observer's coordinate time alone.
detail_labels: x, ct, A, B, tau, t
avoid: A free-floating clock face, making coordinate time look invariant, excessive competing paths, or placing the worldline at 45 degrees unless discussing light where proper time vanishes.
icon_caption: Ticks along a timelike worldline represent accumulated proper time tau.
detail_caption: The clock ticks belong to the path from A to B; the faint coordinate-time projection is a frame-dependent comparison.

label: Four-vectors
dominant_mnemonic: One spacetime object with four linked components
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to one spacetime arrow A^mu and a compact four-slot component stack. The icon should read as one object, not four independent arrows.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a spacetime arrow A^mu drawn from an origin to a generic event-like point away from 45 degrees, with dashed time and space projections feeding into a grouped four-component column. Add a small invariant-length marker or metric tile nearby to show that the components belong to one Lorentz-transforming object with a shared invariant structure.
physics_idea: A four-vector is one geometric object whose components transform together under Lorentz transformations while preserving invariant inner products.
avoid: A dense component equation, ordinary 3D perspective, four unrelated arrows, or placing the example arrow accidentally on a lightlike 45-degree direction.
icon_caption: One spacetime arrow and a component column represent a single four-vector.
detail_caption: The arrow is one Lorentz-transforming object, while the parenthesized entries indicate linked components, not separate vectors.

label: Position four-vector
dominant_mnemonic: Event position packaged as a four-vector from the origin
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to x/ct axes, one red event P away from 45 degrees, and one blue arrow x^mu from the origin to P.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a spacetime coordinate diagram with an origin event O, an arbitrary event P at a non-45-degree location, and a bold arrow x^mu from O to P. Add dashed projections to ct and x axes and a small component stack (ct, x, y, z) to show the event coordinates packaged as one four-vector. The coordinate projections are explanatory; the origin-to-event vector is central.
physics_idea: The position four-vector packages the spacetime coordinates of one event into a four-vector; differences of such vectors determine intervals.
avoid: Multiple events as the main focus, velocity or momentum arrows, full coordinate algebra, or placing P on a 45-degree light ray.
icon_caption: The position four-vector points from the origin event O to event P.
detail_caption: Dashed projections and the coordinate pair indicate that x^mu packages the event coordinates in the spacetime diagram.

label: Velocity four-vector
dominant_mnemonic: Tangent to a timelike worldline per unit proper time
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a curved timelike worldline, one red event, and a green tangent arrow u^mu at that event.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a timelike worldline in x/ct with two nearby tick marks labelled tau and tau + d tau. At the central event draw a bold tangent vector u^mu that points inside the light cone and is not at 45 degrees. Add a faint dx^mu displacement between neighbouring points to imply u^mu = dx^mu/dtau without making the equation dominate.
physics_idea: The velocity four-vector is the derivative of position four-vector with respect to proper time, so it is naturally shown as a tangent to the worldline.
detail_labels: x, ct, u^mu, dx^mu, d tau
avoid: A horizontal ordinary velocity arrow, speedometer imagery, making acceleration curvature the main topic, or drawing the tangent as lightlike at 45 degrees for a massive particle.
icon_caption: A green tangent vector at a worldline event represents four-velocity.
detail_caption: Nearby proper-time ticks suggest u^mu = dx^mu/dtau: the four-velocity is tangent to a timelike worldline.

label: Momentum four-vector
dominant_mnemonic: Energy and momentum as one timelike four-vector
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to one bold p^mu arrow with a split badge showing E/c as the time component and p as the space component.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a timelike four-vector p^mu in spacetime, paired with a compact component badge whose upper slot is E/c and lower spatial slot is bold p. Add a faint parallel u^mu arrow behind it and a small mass marker m to suggest p^mu = m u^mu for a massive particle. The p^mu arrow should sit inside the light cone rather than on a 45-degree light ray.
physics_idea: The momentum four-vector combines energy and three-momentum into one relativistic object, proportional to the velocity four-vector for a massive particle.
avoid: A Newtonian-only momentum arrow, separate unrelated energy and momentum icons, a long equation, or drawing massive momentum as lightlike.
icon_caption: The four-momentum arrow combines energy and spatial momentum.
detail_caption: The component badge separates E/c and p while the faint u^mu cue recalls p^mu = m u^mu for a massive particle.

label: Mass-energy equivalence
dominant_mnemonic: Rest energy equals mass times c squared
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to the iconic equation E=mc^2 in a bold equation tile, with rest energy as a secondary cue.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a large E=mc^2 equation tile connected to a compact invariant-norm relation p_mu p^mu = m^2 c^2 and a rest-frame note p=0. The equation should remain the visual center, with the derivation cue explaining that it comes from the four-momentum norm.
physics_idea: Mass-energy equivalence is the rest-frame consequence of the relativistic energy-momentum relation.
detail_labels: E=mc^2, p_mu p^mu = m^2 c^2, p=0
avoid: A generic explosion, nuclear imagery as the main motif, implying total energy is always just mc^2, or separating mass and energy as unrelated quantities.
icon_caption: The iconic equation marks rest energy as mass times c squared.
detail_caption: The invariant four-momentum norm reduces to E=mc^2 in the rest frame.

label: Action principle
dominant_mnemonic: Physical history selected by stationary action
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to endpoints A and B, one bold blue stationary path labelled S, and two faint nearby varied paths.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as fixed endpoints A and B with a family of nearby varied paths between them. The physical path is highlighted in blue and labelled stationary S, while grey neighbouring paths carry small variation arrows delta x toward it. Add a subtle flat-bottom or level contour cue near S to communicate stationary rather than shortest. The image should feel like variation of histories, not a maze.
physics_idea: The action principle says the physical path is the one for which the action is stationary under small variations with fixed endpoints.
avoid: A maze, tangled spaghetti paths, force arrows, or any minimum-distance cue that erases the stationary-not-necessarily-shortest idea.
icon_caption: A highlighted path between fixed endpoints is compared with nearby variations.
detail_caption: The blue path is selected by stationary action; nearby grey paths and delta x cues show variation, not shortest distance.

label: Lagrangian
dominant_mnemonic: Local dynamical rule accumulated into action
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a short trajectory with one highlighted local tile L feeding into a larger action tile S.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a path or field-history strip carrying several small local L tiles along it, each representing the Lagrangian evaluated locally. Use arrows from those tiles into an integral/action container S, optionally marked integral L dt, to show accumulation into the action. Keep it visually local-to-global and distinct from phase-space Hamiltonian pictures.
physics_idea: The Lagrangian is the local dynamical quantity whose integral over a path gives the action used in the variational principle.
avoid: A full formula for L, Hamiltonian q/p axes, energy-conservation imagery, or making L look like a conserved quantity.
icon_caption: Local Lagrangian tiles accumulate into the action S.
detail_caption: Repeated L values along a history feed the action, expressing S as the integral of a local dynamical rule.

label: Euler-Lagrange equations
dominant_mnemonic: Stationary variation becoming equations of motion
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a blue path, a nearby varied path with delta mark, and an arrow into an E-L equation tile.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a three-stage flow: fixed endpoints with varied paths, a central stationary-action condition delta S = 0, and a clean trajectory emerging from an Euler-Lagrange tile. The E-L tile can contain a compact label rather than the full differential equation. The viewer should read variation first, equation second, motion third.
physics_idea: Euler-Lagrange equations are the equations of motion obtained by requiring the action to be stationary under variations of the path or field.
avoid: Writing the full differential equation as the main graphic, cluttered calculus notation, generic balance scales, or losing the fixed-endpoint variation idea.
icon_caption: A varied path leads to an Euler-Lagrange equation tile.
detail_caption: The graphic reads from fixed-endpoint variations through delta S = 0 to the equations of motion.

label: Hamiltonian formalism
dominant_mnemonic: Time evolution as flow through phase space
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to q and p axes, one curved phase-space trajectory with arrowheads, and a compact H tile.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a phase-space portrait with q horizontal and p vertical, one or two Hamiltonian contour curves, and a green flow line with several arrowheads generated by H. Include a small H tile or contour label. Make the visual vocabulary clearly phase-space, not spacetime: no ct axis and no worldlines.
physics_idea: Hamiltonian formalism rewrites dynamics in terms of coordinates and canonical momenta, with the Hamiltonian generating flow through phase space.
avoid: Spacetime x/ct axes, Lagrangian path variations, Legendre-transform algebra, or a single particle track with no phase-space meaning.
icon_caption: A curve in q-p phase space is generated by H.
detail_caption: Hamiltonian contours and arrowheads show time evolution as flow through phase space, not motion through spacetime.

label: Canonical momentum
dominant_mnemonic: Momentum paired with a coordinate through the Lagrangian
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a q coordinate mark with its paired p arrow and a small L-to-p cue.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a coordinate q on a configuration axis with velocity qdot indicated locally, an L tile that depends on qdot, and an arrow from L through a compact derivative tag dL/dqdot into the conjugate momentum p. Pair q and p visually as canonical partners while keeping p distinct from relativistic four-momentum.
physics_idea: Canonical momentum is the momentum conjugate to a coordinate, defined from how the Lagrangian depends on the corresponding velocity.
avoid: Relativistic four-momentum as the main image, a simple free-particle track, a full partial-derivative formula, or phase-space flow that belongs to Hamiltonian formalism.
icon_caption: The coordinate q is paired with a conjugate momentum p.
detail_caption: The Lagrangian and derivative tile show that canonical momentum is derived from the velocity dependence of L.

label: Noether's theorem
dominant_mnemonic: Continuous symmetry producing conserved quantity
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a circular symmetry arrow around S pointing to a gold conserved Q badge.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a continuous symmetry transformation acting on the action S, shown by a smooth circular or sliding arrow, with the transformed action still marked unchanged. From that unchanged-action badge draw a clear implication arrow to a locked conserved quantity Q. Optionally include one tiny example token such as time shift to energy, but only if it does not crowd the theorem graphic.
physics_idea: Noether's theorem links every continuous symmetry of the action to a conserved quantity, such as energy, momentum, or charge.
avoid: A generic theorem scroll, too many examples, discrete-only symmetry imagery, or making conservation appear without the symmetry-to-action link.
icon_caption: A continuous symmetry of S points to a conserved Q.
detail_caption: The unchanged action under a smooth transformation leads to a conserved quantity, the core Noether relation.

label: Scalar field
dominant_mnemonic: One value assigned to each spacetime point
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to an x/ct grid with several filled discs of varying size or colour labelled phi.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a spacetime grid with field values phi(x) sampled at many events. Each sample is non-directional: use coloured discs, heights, or contour shading, varying smoothly across the grid. Include one highlighted event with its scalar value callout phi(x0). The absence of arrows is part of the lesson.
physics_idea: A scalar field assigns one observer-independent value to every spacetime event; it is a function over spacetime rather than a single particle coordinate.
avoid: Vector arrows, flow lines, particle tracks, or any cue implying a direction at each point.
icon_caption: Different-sized discs show one scalar value at each spacetime point.
detail_caption: The samples represent a scalar function phi(x): values vary over spacetime but carry no direction.

label: Vector field
dominant_mnemonic: A vector attached to every spacetime point
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to an x/ct grid with several blue arrows anchored at grid points and labelled A_mu.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a spacetime grid whose events each carry an anchored vector arrow A_mu(x). Let arrow directions and lengths vary smoothly across the grid, and show faint anchor dots underneath so the field reads as many local vectors, not one free vector. A small component stack at one event may clarify that each arrow has components.
physics_idea: A vector field assigns vector components to every spacetime event, transforming covariantly under Lorentz transformations.
avoid: A single isolated four-vector, scalar dots only, fluid-flow imagery without spacetime anchors, or arrows detached from events.
icon_caption: Anchored arrows show a vector attached to each spacetime point.
detail_caption: The varying arrows represent A_mu(x), a vector field with components defined locally at each event.

label: Field Lagrangian
dominant_mnemonic: Local density over spacetime accumulated into field action
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a spacetime grid with one highlighted local cell L feeding into an action tile S.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a spacetime region subdivided into small cells, several of which carry local density labels mathcal L. Use arrows from the cells into an integral/action container S with a compact d4x label, showing that field action is an integral over spacetime volume rather than over a particle path. Keep the grid/region central.
physics_idea: A field Lagrangian is a local density at spacetime points whose integral over spacetime gives the action for fields.
avoid: Particle path action as the main motif, phase-space q/p axes, or a full field-theory formula.
icon_caption: Local L density cells over spacetime feed the field action S.
detail_caption: The d^4x cue shows that field action integrates a Lagrangian density over spacetime volume, not along a particle path.

label: Field equations
dominant_mnemonic: Local differential rule propagating field values
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a spacetime grid, one central highlighted field value, neighbour arrows into it, and a compact field-eq tile.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a local stencil on a spacetime grid: neighbouring field values feed a central event through short arrows, then an equation tile produces the next layer of field values or a smooth wavefront. This should communicate local differential evolution. Use generic phi or field labels rather than Maxwell-specific E and B.
physics_idea: Field equations are local differential equations for fields, obtained by applying Euler-Lagrange variation to the field Lagrangian.
avoid: One particle trajectory, Maxwell-specific notation as the main image, a long differential equation, or nonlocal arrows from distant points.
icon_caption: Neighbouring field values feed a local field-equation tile.
detail_caption: The stencil-like arrows show local differential evolution: nearby field values determine propagation through a field equation.

label: Vector potential \(A_\mu\)
dominant_mnemonic: Potential field whose derivatives make electromagnetic field
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a spacetime grid with several A_mu arrows and a small derivative arrow toward an F tile.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a vector field A_mu(x) on a spacetime grid, with a highlighted local patch where neighbouring A arrows are compared by derivative/curl marks. Feed that local derivative operation into a compact F_mu_nu tile. The potential field remains visually primary; F is the constructed observable object.
physics_idea: The vector potential is a relativistic vector field defined at each event; the observable electromagnetic field is derived from its spacetime derivatives.
avoid: Electric and magnetic fields as the main image, gauge-fixing selectors, scalar-potential-only imagery, or implying A_mu itself is directly unique and observable.
icon_caption: A vector-potential field feeds a derivative operation to produce F.
detail_caption: A local patch of A_mu values is differentiated to build the electromagnetic field tensor F_mu_nu.

label: Field tensor \(F_{\mu\nu}\)
dominant_mnemonic: Antisymmetric tensor containing electric and magnetic fields
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a bold antisymmetric F_mu_nu tile with opposite off-diagonal cells shaded and small E/B cues.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a readable 4x4 antisymmetric matrix-like tile labelled F_mu_nu, with zero diagonal hinted, opposite off-diagonal signs paired, and coloured component groups mapping to E and B. Add small outgoing E arrows and B loop glyphs from the relevant matrix regions. The matrix should teach unification without becoming a dense component table.
physics_idea: The electromagnetic field tensor is an antisymmetric covariant object built from derivatives of A_mu and containing the electric and magnetic field components.
avoid: Separate unrelated E and B pictures, a generic square grid with no antisymmetry cue, or a fully written component matrix too small to read.
icon_caption: An F tensor badge contains electric and magnetic component cues.
detail_caption: The antisymmetric matrix organizes E and B as components of the same relativistic field tensor.

label: Electric field
dominant_mnemonic: Radial force field on charges at rest
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a positive charge with blue radial E arrows pointing outward.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a positive source charge with radial electric field lines, plus a small positive test charge at rest and a force arrow aligned with the local E direction. If a negative source is hinted, keep it secondary. The key lesson is force per unit charge on a rest charge in the chosen frame.
physics_idea: The electric field is the component of the electromagnetic field that acts on charges at rest in a chosen inertial frame.
avoid: Magnetic loops, moving-charge/current motifs, tensor notation as the main image, or field lines that swirl like B rather than radiating from charge.
icon_caption: Radial E arrows show the electric field of a positive charge.
detail_caption: A rest test charge experiences a force along the local electric field direction.

label: Magnetic field
dominant_mnemonic: Circulating field tied to moving charges and currents
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a current arrow or moving charge with green circular B arrows around it.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a straight current or moving charge with concentric circular magnetic field arrows around the direction of motion. Add a small moving test charge with a sideways deflection arrow to show magnetic action depends on motion. Keep the source motion/current visually explicit so B is not mistaken for a static-charge radial field.
physics_idea: The magnetic field is the spatial component of the electromagnetic field associated with moving charges and currents, frame-dependent together with E.
avoid: Radial electric field lines, static charge-only pictures, making B independent of motion, or replacing the concept with a full tensor diagram.
icon_caption: Circular B arrows wrap around a moving charge or current.
detail_caption: The source motion/current sets the magnetic field direction, and a moving test charge receives a sideways force.

label: Electromagnetic field
dominant_mnemonic: Unified relativistic field with E and B components
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to one central F_mu_nu badge with a blue E arrow and green B loop emerging from it.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a single electromagnetic field tensor object F_mu_nu on a light spacetime grid, with E arrows and B loops shown as frame-dependent component projections of that one object. Include a subtle frame marker to imply E/B split depends on observer, while F remains the unified field.
physics_idea: The electromagnetic field unifies electric and magnetic fields in the field tensor; E and B are frame-dependent components of one relativistic object.
avoid: Two disconnected electric and magnetic diagrams, source charges as the focus, Maxwell equations as the main graphic, or implying E and B are independent fields.
icon_caption: One F object gives rise to E and B component views.
detail_caption: The tensor F_mu_nu is the unified electromagnetic field; E and B are frame-dependent components of it.

label: Maxwell's equations
dominant_mnemonic: Local equations linking sources, fields, and waves
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a source four-current J feeding a field tensor F, with a small outgoing wavefront.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a local spacetime patch where charge-current J_mu feeds into a compact Maxwell operator acting on F_mu_nu, producing both nearby field lines and a propagating wavefront. Use a small partial symbol to show locality, but do not write the full equations. The composition should read source -> local field law -> propagation.
physics_idea: Maxwell's equations locally relate electromagnetic fields to charge-current sources and imply propagating electromagnetic waves.
avoid: Four separate textbook panels, full differential equations, only a static charge field, or a wave picture with no source/law relation.
icon_caption: A source J feeds a field F, with outgoing wave marks.
detail_caption: Maxwell equations locally relate source current to the electromagnetic field and allow propagating waves.

label: Lorenz gauge
dominant_mnemonic: Covariant divergence condition selecting a convenient potential
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to A_mu arrows passing through a Lorenz gauge filter and emerging as one cleaner A_mu, with a small partial dot A = 0 tag.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a before/after potential-field panel: several gauge-equivalent A_mu patterns enter a covariant gauge selector labelled partial_mu A^mu = 0, and one clean representative exits. Keep a faint unchanged F_mu_nu tile behind both sides to show the physical electromagnetic field is not changed. The word Lorenz should be spelled distinctly from Lorentz.
physics_idea: The Lorenz gauge is a covariant condition on the vector potential that simplifies Maxwell's equations without changing the physical electromagnetic field.
avoid: Confusing Lorenz gauge with Lorentz transformations, changing F, or making the gauge condition look like a new force law.
icon_caption: Potential representatives pass through a Lorenz gauge selector.
detail_caption: The condition partial_mu A^mu = 0 selects a convenient potential representative while leaving F unchanged.

label: Gauge invariance
dominant_mnemonic: Equivalent potentials producing the same physical field
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to two A_mu representatives connected by a Lambda gauge arrow, both pointing to the same F tile.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as two potential representatives A_mu and A_mu + partial_mu Lambda on parallel lanes, joined by a gauge-transformation arrow labelled Lambda. Both representatives feed into one identical F_mu_nu tile, which is visually locked or highlighted as unchanged. The same-field endpoint is the teaching focus.
physics_idea: Gauge invariance is the freedom to change the vector potential by a gauge transformation without changing the physical electromagnetic field.
avoid: Changing F, making the gauge transformation look like a Lorentz coordinate transformation, or using only abstract symmetry arrows with no potential.
icon_caption: Two gauge-related potentials point to the same F.
detail_caption: A_mu and A_mu + partial_mu Lambda are different potential representatives that produce the same physical field tensor.

label: Four-current
dominant_mnemonic: Charge density and current density as one source four-vector
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a J^mu badge split into rho on the time side and J on the space side.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a spacetime source field: charge-density dots in a small volume and current arrows flowing through it, packaged into one four-vector badge J^mu with rho c as the time component and J as the spatial component. Add a small axis inset to show it is a relativistic source object.
physics_idea: The four-current combines charge density and current density into one relativistic source four-vector for electromagnetism.
detail_labels: J^mu, rho c, J, x, ct
avoid: A plain electric source charge only, separate unrelated charge and current icons, fluid flow without relativistic packaging, or too much continuity-equation detail.
icon_caption: A J^mu badge combines charge density and current.
detail_caption: Charge dots and current arrows are packaged as the four-current source J^mu, with density and flux components.

label: Minimal coupling
dominant_mnemonic: Free momentum replaced by gauge-covariant momentum
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to p entering a coupling node with A_mu and exiting as p - eA.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a free charged-particle line with momentum p entering a region carrying vector-potential arrows A_mu. Through a coupling node labelled e, the outgoing canonical/kinetic combination is shown as p - eA or p_mu - e A_mu. Add a faint gauge-covariant path cue so the image reads interaction through the potential rather than an ordinary force push.
physics_idea: Minimal coupling introduces electromagnetic interaction by replacing ordinary momentum with a gauge-covariant combination involving the vector potential.
detail_labels: p_mu, A_mu, e, p_mu - e A_mu
avoid: A generic force arrow, Maxwell equations, a long Lagrangian, or making A_mu look like a one-off external shove instead of a field-coupling rule.
icon_caption: Free momentum p enters an A-dependent coupling rule.
detail_caption: Minimal coupling replaces ordinary momentum with p_mu - e A_mu, introducing electromagnetic interaction through the potential.

label: Lorentz force law
dominant_mnemonic: Field tensor and velocity changing particle momentum
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a charged particle path with velocity u, nearby F tile, and red momentum-change arrow dp.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a charged particle worldline with four-velocity u^mu passing through a local electromagnetic field tensor F_mu_nu. Draw an arrow from F and u into a red change in four-momentum dp^mu/dtau at the particle. If E/B hints are present, keep them secondary to the covariant F-u-to-dp structure.
physics_idea: The Lorentz force law gives the relativistic change of a charged particle's momentum due to the electromagnetic field tensor and its velocity.
avoid: A Newtonian-only electric force picture, radiation reaction recoil, separate E and B diagrams as the main motif, or a force arrow disconnected from velocity.
icon_caption: A particle with velocity u in field F receives a momentum change dp.
detail_caption: The force law combines charge, field tensor, and four-velocity to determine dp^mu/dtau.

label: Charge conservation
dominant_mnemonic: Local continuity of charge and current
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a small spacetime box with charge dots and current arrows crossing the boundary, labelled J.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a local spacetime volume containing charge density rho and boundary current arrows J. Show a before/after or inflow/outflow balance with a compact continuity tag partial_mu J^mu = 0. The lesson is local bookkeeping: changes inside are exactly accounted for by current through the boundary.
physics_idea: Charge conservation is local bookkeeping: charge cannot appear or vanish; changes in density are balanced by current flow.
avoid: A global battery icon, isolated static charge, charge disappearing, or conservation shown only as a whole-universe total with no local flux.
icon_caption: Charge dots leave a local box only through current arrows.
detail_caption: The continuity equation says local charge changes are balanced by current flow through the boundary.

label: Energy density of EM field
dominant_mnemonic: Local energy stored in electric and magnetic fields
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to one glowing field cell containing E and B glyphs plus a gold energy-density fill labelled u.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a small grid of local electromagnetic field cells, each containing blue E and green B cues and a gold fill level representing energy density u. Highlight one cell with a callout u_EM while neighbouring cells show different fill levels. Keep this about stored local density, not transport.
physics_idea: The electromagnetic field carries local energy density built from electric and magnetic field strengths.
avoid: A battery, particle kinetic energy, Poynting-flow arrows as the main cue, or stress-tensor face arrows.
icon_caption: A field cell stores energy density u in E and B.
detail_caption: Neighbouring cells with E/B glyphs indicate that electromagnetic energy density is local and spatially distributed.

label: Momentum density (Poynting vector)
dominant_mnemonic: Energy-momentum flow from crossed E and B fields
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to crossed E and B arrows producing a bold gold S arrow.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a local field region with E and B perpendicular glyphs at several points, each producing parallel Poynting arrows S in the propagation/flow direction. Add one compact cross-product cue E x B -> S if readable. The S arrows should clearly indicate energy-momentum flux, distinct from static energy density.
physics_idea: The Poynting vector represents the flow of electromagnetic energy and momentum carried by the field.
detail_labels: E, B, S, E x B
avoid: Static energy-density cells, stress tensor grids, a generic wave with no E/B cross-product cue, or momentum flow detached from fields.
icon_caption: Crossed E and B arrows produce a Poynting vector S.
detail_caption: Repeated E-B-S motifs show electromagnetic energy-momentum flow density through the field region.

label: Stress-energy of EM field
dominant_mnemonic: Electromagnetic density, flux, and stress in one tensor
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a tensor block T_EM with E/B glyphs inside and arrows on its faces.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as an electromagnetic stress-energy tensor block T_EM with separate visual regions for energy density, Poynting flux, and stresses: gold fill inside, gold flow arrow through one face, and blue/green pressure or shear arrows on other faces. Include tiny E/B glyphs to identify the field source of the tensor.
physics_idea: The electromagnetic stress-energy tensor summarizes energy density, momentum flow, and mechanical stresses carried by the electromagnetic field.
detail_labels: T_EM, u, S, stress, E, B
avoid: Only an energy-density picture, only a Poynting vector, a full component matrix, or mechanical stress imagery with no electromagnetic origin.
icon_caption: A T_EM block combines field glyphs with face arrows.
detail_caption: The tensor block summarizes energy density, Poynting flux, and electromagnetic stresses in one object.

label: Energy--momentum tensor
dominant_mnemonic: Local density and flux of energy-momentum
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a T^munu tensor tile with one density cell and one flux cell highlighted, plus boundary arrows.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a spacetime volume with a nearby T^munu tile whose rows/columns are hinted as energy-momentum components. Arrows crossing the volume boundary show fluxes, while a compact partial_mu T^munu = 0 tag states local conservation. Keep it generic, not specifically electromagnetic, so it can cover matter and fields.
physics_idea: The energy-momentum tensor packages energy density, momentum density, fluxes, and stresses; its divergence expresses local conservation.
avoid: A purely electromagnetic E/B icon, a dense 4x4 matrix, global-only conservation imagery, or hiding the local flux-through-boundary idea.
icon_caption: A T^munu tile highlights density and flux cells.
detail_caption: Boundary arrows and the divergence condition represent local conservation of energy and momentum.

label: Electromagnetic waves
dominant_mnemonic: Coupled E and B oscillations propagating at light speed
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to blue E and green B sinusoidal waves travelling together with a gold propagation arrow c.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a propagating electromagnetic wave packet with mutually perpendicular E and B oscillations, wavefront marks, a direction arrow labelled c, and a smaller Poynting arrow S in the same propagation direction. Use phase alignment and colour to show the fields are coupled parts of one wave.
physics_idea: Electromagnetic waves are self-propagating coupled electric and magnetic field oscillations travelling at light speed.
avoid: A single scalar wave, water-wave imagery, source charges as the focus, or waves where E and B appear unrelated.
icon_caption: Coupled E and B waves propagate in the c direction.
detail_caption: Wavefront marks and the S arrow show that electromagnetic waves carry energy-momentum at light speed.

label: Wave equation
dominant_mnemonic: Operator law producing propagating solutions
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to a box operator acting on A_mu and producing an outgoing sinusoidal wave.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a compact differential-operator tile square/box acting on A_mu. Show two outputs: with source J_mu it links to a sourced disturbance, and in the source-free branch it produces a clean propagating wave. Keep the operator-to-solution relationship central and avoid writing a full PDE.
physics_idea: The wave equation describes how the potential or field propagates, with source terms producing waves and source-free regions supporting free propagation.
avoid: A generic sine wave with no operator cue, Maxwell source law alone, dense differential notation, or a particle trajectory.
icon_caption: A wave operator acting on A produces a propagating wave.
detail_caption: The source branch and free-wave branch express how the wave equation connects sources, potentials, and propagation.

label: Radiation reaction
dominant_mnemonic: Accelerating charge emits radiation and recoils
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to an accelerating charge, outgoing wavefronts, and a small recoil arrow opposite emitted momentum.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a charged particle on a curved accelerated path, with outgoing electromagnetic wavefronts carrying a gold energy-momentum arrow away. Add a red recoil/back-reaction arrow on the charge and a slightly altered subsequent path. The viewer should see emission causes a self-effect, not just ordinary Lorentz-force deflection.
physics_idea: Radiation reaction is the back-reaction on an accelerating charge because emitted electromagnetic waves carry away energy and momentum.
avoid: A stationary charge field, simple external Lorentz force, waves without recoil, or recoil with no emitted energy-momentum.
icon_caption: An accelerating charge emits waves and recoils.
detail_caption: Radiated energy-momentum leaves in the wave, producing a back-reaction on the charge trajectory.

label: Lorentz invariance
dominant_mnemonic: Same physical law in every inertial frame
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to unprimed and primed frames feeding one unchanged law tile with a check mark.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as two inertial coordinate systems, black x/ct and blue tilted x'/ct', both pointing to the same compact law/equation tile. Add an invariant badge or unchanged-form ring around the tile. If an example event appears, place it generically away from 45 degrees and keep it secondary; the law's unchanged form is the subject.
physics_idea: Lorentz invariance means the laws or equations of physics keep the same form under Lorentz transformations.
detail_labels: x, ct, x', ct', invariant law
avoid: A mere coordinate transformation with no unchanged law, gauge invariance imagery, a specific particle trajectory as the main object, or accidental 45-degree event placement.
icon_caption: Primed and unprimed frames feed the same law tile.
detail_caption: Lorentz invariance means the equation keeps its form under changes between inertial frames.

label: Gauge fixing
dominant_mnemonic: Choosing one representative from gauge-equivalent potentials
icon_design: Simplify the detailed diagram to several faint A_mu representatives passing through a selector into one bold chosen A_mu, with F unchanged in the background.
detail_design: Design the detailed graphic as a bundle of gauge-equivalent potential representatives A_mu, A'_mu, and A''_mu that all correspond to the same faint F_mu_nu tile. A grey selector or gauge condition chooses one clean representative for calculation. The unchanged F tile should remain visible behind both sides so gauge fixing reads as choice of description, not change of physics.
physics_idea: Gauge fixing chooses one convenient representative among many gauge-equivalent vector potentials that describe the same electromagnetic field.
avoid: Confusing gauge fixing with Lorenz gauge only, changing F, making it look like a dynamical force, or losing the many-equivalent-to-one-chosen structure.
icon_caption: Many gauge-equivalent potentials pass through a selector to one A.
detail_caption: Gauge fixing chooses one convenient potential representative while the physical field F remains unchanged.
